title: Dog Eyelashes Growing Inward: Causes, Symptoms, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's eyelash growing inward? Learn about distichiasis, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
What is Distichiasis and Inward-Growing Eyelashes?

When a dog has eyelashes growing toward the eye, it is typically one of two conditions: distichiasis or ectopic cilia. Distichiasis involves extra hairs growing from the eyelid glands, while ectopic cilia are hairs that poke through the inner lining of the eyelid. Both conditions cause the hair to rub against the cornea, the clear front part of the eye, leading to significant discomfort and irritation.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for inward-growing eyelashes is Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a painful condition that requires prompt veterinary attention. If left untreated, the constant friction can cause corneal ulcers, infections, or permanent scarring that may impair your dog's vision. You should aim to have your dog seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours, or sooner if the eye appears cloudy or if your dog is unable to open it.

Why a Photo Helps with Triage

Capturing a clear, close-up photo of the affected eye can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. High-quality images allow a professional to see the exact placement of the stray hairs and check for signs of secondary damage, such as corneal cloudiness or significant discharge. This information helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be fit into the schedule and ensures they are prepared with the right diagnostic tools when you arrive.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of ingrown eyelashes in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include squinting, excessive tearing (epiphora), redness in the white of the eye, and your dog rubbing their face or pawing at their eye.

faqs.question: Can I pluck the eyelash myself at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pluck or trim these hairs at home. Doing so can cause severe injury to the eye or lead to a serious infection. Professional veterinary tools and often sedation are needed for safe removal.

faqs.question: How do veterinarians treat inward-growing eyelashes?
faqs.answer: Treatment varies based on severity but may include cryotherapy (freezing the follicles), surgery to remove the affected tissue, or electrolysis to permanently stop hair growth.
